title: Agentic Support Platform — System Design
description: Design the platform for customer-support / CX agents that act in real time — answering questions and taking transactional actions (refund, cancel, reschedule) on the customer's behalf, mid-conversation, and handing off to a human when needed. It introduces an axis the rest of the series doesn't have: every other vertical gates BEFORE an async action, but support gates INSIDE a live loop under a turn-taking latency budget. It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations and adds two vertical-specific things: a product/policy-KB-plus-scoped-account grounding corpus, and an in-loop action gate that classifies each action by reversibility and decides auto-execute vs approve vs escalate — with transactional side effects made safe by idempotency and rollback.

nodes.label: Customer
nodes.description: The person in a live conversation; the agent acts on their behalf and hands off when needed.

nodes.label: Human Agent
nodes.description: Support agent who takes escalations and approves high-risk actions, with conversation context.

nodes.label: Live Channel
nodes.description: Chat, voice, or messaging surface where the real-time conversation happens.

nodes.label: Support API
nodes.description: Authenticates the session, resolves the customer's scope, routes to the conversation loop.

nodes.label: Conversation Agent
nodes.description: Drives the real-time turn loop: understand, retrieve, decide deflect/act/escalate.

nodes.label: Inference Backend
nodes.description: Shared low-latency LLM serving tier (from the Foundations platform).

nodes.label: Product / Policy KB
nodes.description: Grounding corpus: product docs, help articles, and refund/return policy.

nodes.label: Account / Order Record
nodes.description: The customer's account, orders, and entitlements; the read and action surface (scoped).

nodes.label: In-Loop Action Gate
nodes.description: Classifies an action by reversibility/risk and decides auto-execute, approve, or escalate — inside the live turn.

nodes.label: Transaction Engine
nodes.description: Executes refunds, cancels, reschedules idempotently, with compensating actions for rollback.

nodes.label: Payments / Commerce
nodes.description: Payment and order systems for refunds and (optionally) agentic checkout.

nodes.label: Escalation Router
nodes.description: Routes deflect-vs-escalate and hands a conversation to a human with full context.

nodes.label: Session / Durable State
nodes.description: Live conversation state plus a durable log so an action resumes exactly-once.

nodes.label: Policy & Safety
nodes.description: Entitlement/policy checks, PII handling, and screening of untrusted customer input.

nodes.label: Identity & Token Broker
nodes.description: Scoped, delegated authority to act on the customer's behalf; ties approval to a human agent.

nodes.label: Audit Record
nodes.description: Append-only record: turns, retrieved facts, action decisions, approvals, transactions, handoffs.

nodes.label: Observability & Eval
nodes.description: Trajectories, latency/CSAT/resolution metrics, deflection and action-correctness eval.

functional: Hold a real-time conversation across chat/voice/messaging channels.
Answer grounded in product/policy docs and the customer's scoped account/order record.
Take transactional actions (refund, cancel, reschedule) on the customer's behalf within the conversation.
Decide, per turn, whether to deflect (answer), act, or escalate to a human.
Hand off to a human agent with full conversation context when needed.
nonFunctional: Real-time: each turn must respond within a latency budget; the gate runs inside the loop, not before it.
Transactional side effects are idempotent and reversible (compensating actions for rollback).
Higher-risk / less-reversible actions require human approval; low-risk reversible ones can auto-execute.
Act only with scoped, delegated authority; customer input is untrusted.
Auditable: every turn, action decision, approval, transaction, and handoff is recorded.

capacity.label: Turn latency
capacity.note: Each turn (retrieve + reason + maybe act) must fit a conversational latency budget.

capacity.label: Action autonomy
capacity.note: Low-risk reversible actions auto-execute; high-risk / hard-to-reverse ones need human approval.

capacity.label: Side effects
capacity.note: Refunds/cancels carry idempotency keys; a retry never double-refunds.

api.description: Process one live turn: understand, retrieve, and decide deflect/act/escalate within the latency budget.
A human agent approves a high-risk action proposed mid-conversation; only then does it execute.
Hand the conversation to a human agent with full context.

dataModel.name: actions
dataModel.note: A transactional action proposed or taken in a conversation.

patterns.name: Agentic loop (ReAct)
patterns.what: A real-time understand -> retrieve -> decide -> respond loop per conversational turn.

patterns.name: Human-in-the-loop approval (gate)
patterns.what: High-risk actions need a human agent's approval mid-conversation.
patterns.whenToUse: Before a hard-to-reverse action on the customer's account.

patterns.name: Bounded autonomy (agent + deterministic gate)
patterns.what: The agent converses and acts freely but a reversibility-tiered gate fences the action.
patterns.whenToUse: Live action-taking with a risk boundary inside the loop.

patterns.name: Durable execution / checkpointing (resume after crash)
patterns.what: Idempotent transactions with compensating actions so a mid-action failure resumes exactly-once.

patterns.name: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)
patterns.what: Scoped, delegated authority to act on the customer's behalf; approval tied to a human agent.

patterns.name: Memory: short-term vs long-term (semantic/episodic/procedural)
patterns.what: Ground replies in product/policy docs and the customer's scoped record.

patterns.name: Prompt-injection defense (CaMeL / Dual-LLM / Plan-then-Execute)
patterns.what: Treat customer messages as untrusted; partition from account-action authority.

patterns.name: LLM-as-judge / trajectory evaluation
patterns.what: Evaluate resolution quality and action correctness, not just CSAT.

steps.title: 1. Naive: A Bot That Answers But Can't Act (the baseline)
description: Start with the classic chatbot: it answers FAQs from a script or the model, but the moment a customer wants something done — a refund, a cancellation — it dead-ends to a human queue.

It deflects but doesn't resolve, so it frustrates customers and offloads every real task. The opposite failure (an agent that freely refunds and cancels) is worse: unlike every other vertical so far, the dangerous action happens live, in the middle of a conversation, on the customer's money and orders.
The rest of the interview turns this into a real-time agent that can safely act inside the conversation, with the gate inside the loop.

view.caption: A bot answers but dead-ends every real task to a human — the deflection-only baseline.

steps.decisionPrompt: What's different about the gate when the risky action happens live, mid-conversation?
whyNow: The deflection-only baseline makes the real-time, in-loop transactional requirement concrete.

recap.after: A bot can answer FAQs in a conversation.
recap.newRisk: It can't resolve anything that needs an action, and a naive 'let it act' version would act live with no controls.

traps.trap: Either deflecting everything to humans or letting the agent act freely.
traps.why: The first doesn't resolve; the second takes irreversible account actions live with no gate.
traps.instead: Let the agent act in the loop, but gate actions by reversibility and risk.

steps.title: 2. Ground in Policy + the Customer's Account
description: The grounding corpus is two-sided: product/policy/help documents (what's allowed) and the customer's own scoped account and order record (what's true for them). A refund answer depends on both the refund policy and this customer's order status.
Account retrieval is scoped to the authenticated customer; customer messages are treated as untrusted input.

view.caption: Replies are grounded in product/policy docs and the customer's own scoped account/order record.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why must an answer be grounded in BOTH the policy and this customer's order?

concepts.definition: Grounding a reply in both the applicable policy and the customer's specific account/order state.
concepts.whyItMatters: A policy-correct answer can still be wrong for this order (out of window, already refunded).
concepts.example: Refund policy allows 30 days, but this order shipped 45 days ago -> not eligible.

whyNow: Grounding determines both what the agent says and which actions are even valid.

recap.after: Replies are grounded in policy and the customer's record.
recap.newRisk: Grounded answers in a live chat must still come back within a conversational latency budget.

steps.title: 3. The Real-Time Loop & Latency Budget
description: Support is interactive, so the agent runs a real-time turn loop — understand, retrieve, reason, respond — and each turn must fit a conversational latency budget. This is the axis the rest of the series doesn't have: latency is a first-class constraint.
Session state is kept live (and durably logged) so the conversation is coherent across turns and survives a process restart.

view.caption: A real-time turn loop (understand, retrieve, reason, respond) runs within a latency budget, with durable session state.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you keep a turn within budget when it may need retrieval, reasoning, and an action?

concepts.definition: A per-turn time limit the agent must respond within to keep a conversation natural.
concepts.whyItMatters: Async verticals optimize throughput; live support optimizes per-turn latency — a different design pressure.
concepts.example: Retrieval + reasoning are bounded so a reply lands in a couple of seconds, streaming partials if needed.

whyNow: The real-time loop is the substrate the in-loop action gate plugs into.

recap.after: Each turn responds within a latency budget over durable session state.
recap.newRisk: Within that live loop, the agent now needs to take real account actions safely.

steps.title: 4. The Gate: Take Actions Inside the Loop, by Reversibility
description: This is the crux, and it's where support diverges from the rest of the series: the gate lives inside the live conversation. When the agent wants to act, an in-loop action gate classifies the action by reversibility and risk — low-risk reversible actions (resend a receipt, a small in-policy refund) auto-execute; high-risk or hard-to-reverse ones (large refund, account change) require a human agent's approval mid-conversation.

Identity grants scoped, delegated authority to act on the customer's behalf. Agentic checkout/commerce is an option here: the same gate authorizes a purchase the agent makes for the customer (where a commerce protocol like ACP carries delegated payment authority).

view.caption: An in-loop gate classifies each action by reversibility: low-risk auto-executes; high-risk needs a human agent's approval.

steps.decisionPrompt: Which actions can the agent auto-execute live, and which must pause for human approval?
patterns: Delegation not impersonation (OAuth token exchange, act claim)

concepts.definition: An in-loop classifier that decides auto-execute vs human-approve vs escalate based on how reversible and risky the action is.
concepts.whyItMatters: It lets the agent resolve common cases instantly while pausing the dangerous, hard-to-reverse ones.
concepts.example: Resend receipt -> auto; refund $1,500 -> human approval; close account -> escalate.

whyNow: The in-loop gate is what lets the agent actually resolve issues without acting recklessly.

recap.before: The agent could only talk, or would act with no control.
recap.after: It acts inside the loop, tiered by reversibility, with approval for risky actions.
recap.newRisk: Live actions can fail mid-flight — they must be idempotent and reversible.

traps.trap: Using one approval rule for every action.
traps.why: It either blocks trivial resolutions or waves through hard-to-reverse ones.
traps.instead: Tier the gate by reversibility and risk; auto only the cheaply-reversible.

steps.title: 5. Transactional Safety: Idempotency & Rollback
description: Acting live means failures happen mid-action: a refund call times out, the session drops, the customer retries. So every action is idempotent (keyed, deduped) and reversible via compensating actions, with a durable log so a crashed turn resumes exactly-once.

This is the Foundations durable-execution discipline applied to a live, customer-facing side effect — and customer input is treated as untrusted so an injected message can't drive an unintended action.

view.caption: Refunds/cancels are idempotent with compensating rollback and a durable log, so a mid-action failure never double-applies.

steps.decisionPrompt: A refund succeeds but the connection drops before confirmation and the customer retries — what prevents a double refund?

concepts.definition: A reversing operation (re-charge, re-book) that undoes a committed side effect when a later step fails.
concepts.whyItMatters: Live transactions can't be wrapped in a single DB transaction; rollback is by compensation.
concepts.example: A refund is issued but the cancel fails -> compensate by reversing the refund.

whyNow: Transactional safety must wrap the action gate before live acting is trustworthy.

recap.before: A failed or retried action could double-apply.
recap.after: Actions are idempotent and reversible via compensation, resumable exactly-once.
recap.newRisk: Some conversations still need a human — handoff must be smooth and contextful.

steps.title: 6. Deflect-vs-Escalate & Human Handoff
description: Not every conversation should be resolved by the agent. A deflect-vs-escalate decision routes routine issues to the agent and hands the rest to a human — on low confidence, an out-of-scope request, an explicit ask, or a rejected high-risk action.

The handoff carries full context (the conversation, what was tried, the customer's record) so the human doesn't restart from zero. Escalation is a first-class outcome, like abstention in the healthcare vertical.

view.caption: A deflect-vs-escalate decision hands the conversation to a human agent with full context when the agent shouldn't resolve it.

steps.decisionPrompt: When should the agent stop trying and hand off, and what must the human receive?

concepts.definition: The per-conversation decision to resolve with the agent (deflect from humans) or route to a human.
concepts.whyItMatters: Over-deflecting frustrates customers; under-deflecting wastes the platform — the threshold is a design choice.
concepts.example: Low confidence or a rejected high-value refund -> escalate with full context.

whyNow: Smooth handoff is what makes bounded autonomy acceptable to customers and agents.

deepDives.title: Escalation as a safe default
points: On low confidence or out-of-scope, escalate rather than guess (cf. healthcare abstention).
Hand off the full transcript, attempted actions, and customer context.
A rejected high-risk action is a natural escalation point.

recap.before: The agent either resolved or dead-ended.
recap.after: It deflects what it should and escalates the rest with context.
recap.newRisk: Quality, compliance, and reputation must be measured across live conversations.

steps.title: 7. Compliance, Audit & Evaluation
description: Operate the platform safely at scale. Every turn, action decision, approval, transaction, and handoff is recorded in an append-only audit; PII is handled under policy and customer input stays untrusted.

Evaluate beyond CSAT: use trajectory eval to measure resolution quality and action correctness, so a high satisfaction score never hides a wrong refund or a missed escalation. Latency and deflection rate are watched alongside.

view.caption: Every turn, decision, transaction, and handoff is audited; eval weights resolution quality and action correctness, not just CSAT.

steps.decisionPrompt: Why is CSAT alone a weak quality metric for an agent that takes real actions?

concepts.definition: Measuring whether the actions the agent took were correct and policy-compliant, not just whether the customer was satisfied.
concepts.whyItMatters: A happy customer who got a refund they weren't owed is still a failure; CSAT misses it.
concepts.example: Track wrong-action rate and missed-escalation rate alongside CSAT and latency.

whyNow: Action-aware evaluation is what keeps a live, acting agent trustworthy.

recap.after: Resolution quality, action correctness, latency, and deflection are evaluated and audited.
recap.newRisk: Other phase-3 verticals add contestability and physical-experiment gates — see the plan.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Agentic Support Platform
finalDesign.description: A live message enters through the support API, which resolves the customer's scope. The conversation agent runs a real-time turn loop within a latency budget, grounded in product/policy docs and the customer's scoped account/order record, over durable session state. When it wants to act, an in-loop action gate classifies the action by reversibility: low-risk reversible actions auto-execute through the transaction engine (idempotent, with compensating rollback); high-risk or hard-to-reverse actions pause for a human agent's approval, with identity granting scoped on-behalf-of authority (and agentic checkout as an option). A deflect-vs-escalate decision hands conversations to a human with full context when the agent shouldn't resolve them. Every turn, decision, transaction, and handoff is audited, and evaluation weights resolution quality and action correctness over raw CSAT. Identity/delegation, durable execution, guardrails, and observability are the shared substrate from Agentic Platform Foundations; uniquely, the gate lives inside a live conversation.

functional.how: A turn loop within a latency budget over durable state.
Policy KB plus the customer's scoped account.

functional.requirement: Transactional actions on the customer's behalf
functional.how: In-loop action gate plus the transaction engine.
Escalation router carries the full conversation.

nonFunctional.how: Per-turn budget; the gate runs inside the loop.

say: Frame the new axis: the gate lives inside a live conversation, not before an async action.
It builds on Agentic Platform Foundations.
Draw the deflect-only bot and the opposite danger of free live action.
Ground in policy and the scoped account, then the real-time turn loop and latency budget.
The reversibility-tiered in-loop action gate, then idempotency/rollback and deflect-vs-escalate handoff.
Action-correctness eval beyond CSAT, plus latency and audit.

expectations: Agent answers grounded questions in a chat.
Knows risky actions need a human.
Real-time loop with a latency budget and scoped account grounding.
Puts the gate inside the live loop and tiers actions by reversibility.
Designs idempotency + compensating rollback for live side effects and contextful handoff.
Evaluates action correctness, not just CSAT; builds on the Foundations substrate.

followUps: How do you keep a turn within budget when it needs retrieval, reasoning, and an action?
How do you classify an action's reversibility to decide auto vs approve?
How do compensating actions roll back a partially-completed multi-step resolution?
How does an in-the-loop gate differ from the before-the-action gates in finance and legal?
